name: TOHA — Hallucination Detection in LLMs with Topological Divergence on Attention Graphs
venue: ACL 2026 (64th Annual Meeting of the Association for Computational Linguistics)
authors: Alexandra Bazarova, Aleksandr Yugay, Andrey Shulga, Alina Ermilova, Andrei Volodichev, Konstantin Polev, Julia Belikova, Rauf Parchiev, Dmitry Simakov, Maxim Savchenko, Andrey Savchenko, Serguei Barannikov, Alexey Zaytsev
dataset_size: N/A — method paper; evaluated on RAGTruth (MS MARCO, CNN/DM), CoQA, SQuAD, XSum, HotpotQA
domain: N/A — method paper (RAG QA, conversational QA, summarization, multi-hop QA)
annotation_cost_per_sample: N/A — method paper; requires only 50–100 annotated validation samples for head selection
signal_type: attention (topological divergence on attention graphs using persistent homology)
white_box_requirements: Full attention maps required from all layers and heads. TOHA computes the Minimal Topological Divergence (MTop-Div) between prompt and response subgraphs derived from attention weight matrices. Specifically, MTop-Div(R,P) = length of the minimal spanning forest (MSF) attaching response tokens R to prompt tokens P after zeroing edge weights between prompt nodes. H_0 homology barcode intervals are summed. This is a training-free graph-structural metric — no learned parameters, but requires attention map access.
supervision: weakly-supervised (training-free in deployment; requires ~50–100 annotated samples only for selecting which attention heads to use via a head-ranking validation procedure)
integration_mode: post-hoc detector — extract attention maps, compute MTop-Div between prompt and response subgraphs, classify with threshold
spectral_signature_overlap: medium. TOHA uses persistent homology (H_0 barcode of attention graphs) rather than eigenvalues or Fourier features. Persistent homology is a topological — not spectral — method. However, there is conceptual overlap: (1) Both TOHA and LapEigvals treat attention maps as weighted graphs and extract mathematical structure from them. (2) The H_0 barcode (connected component lifetimes) is related to the Fiedler vector and second smallest Laplacian eigenvalue in graph theory (both capture graph connectivity). (3) The paper explicitly compares against 'spectral norm' as a baseline in Appendix B.3 and shows MTop-Div outperforms it. Thus spectral methods are a recognized competitor in their own comparison. Despite this relationship, TOHA does NOT compute eigenvalues or apply Fourier analysis — it is topological, not spectral. Overlap with our entropy-trajectory spectral method is LOW (different signal, different mathematical framework).
tested_at_what_length: Standard QA and summarization response lengths; multi-hop QA (HotpotQA) tested but not long-document RAG
reported_value: CoQA: 0.89–0.92 (Mistral/LLaMA); SQuAD: 0.87–0.96; MS MARCO: 0.65–0.76; XSum: 0.66–0.68; HotpotQA: 0.71–0.80. Outperforms supervised methods (ReDEEP, HaloScope) and unsupervised baselines (perplexity, semantic entropy, EigenScore, SelfCheckGPT) on most tasks.
overlapping_benchmark: HotpotQA (our Phase result: 59.5% AUROC with Mistral-7B; TOHA HotpotQA: 0.71–0.80 with Mistral/LLaMA — significantly better). We share Mistral-7B-Instruct as a tested model.
applicability: high — specifically designed for RAG settings, which is exactly our Phase 10 target; outperforms our HotpotQA result; only 50 samples needed for head selection
preemption_risk: MEDIUM for RAG. TOHA targets RAG specifically and achieves strong results with minimal annotation (50 samples) — directly in our Phase 10 scope. It does NOT pre-empt our spectral entropy contribution because: (1) TOHA uses topological divergence on attention graphs, not spectral/frequency features of entropy trajectories. These are mathematically distinct approaches. (2) TOHA is post-hoc (not streaming or trajectory-aware) — cannot localize failure position in a long CoT. (3) TOHA does not handle agentic multi-step settings (Thought→Action→Observation). (4) TOHA's own comparison shows it beats spectral norm — meaning our spectral ENTROPY approach (different signal: H(n) not attention) might be complementary. For Phase 10 agentic RAG, we should CITE TOHA as the RAG baseline and argue our contribution adds: (a) entropy-trajectory dynamics not captured by static attention topology, (b) streaming/step-localization capability, (c) unsupervised Nadler fusion needing zero labels (vs. TOHA's 50-sample head selection).
bracha_alignment: High. TOHA's head-selection step uses 50–100 validation samples — a natural target for Bracha's PPI (Prediction-Powered Inference) framework, which could validate or calibrate head selection under label noise. Her LTT/conformal machinery could provide finite-sample guarantees on the MTop-Div threshold, which TOHA currently lacks.
ofir_alignment: High. MTop-Div on attention graphs is a topological complement to Ofir's spectral graph methods (VSDE/STG). Both operate on the graph induced by attention weights; Ofir's spectral approach extracts the leading eigenstructure while TOHA extracts H_0 barcode. These are dual perspectives on the same graph — a natural multi-view fusion opportunity that connects directly to Ofir's multi-view kernel framework.
notes: TOHA is approximately 7x faster than SelfCheckGPT (one additional generation vs. 10–20) and over 70x faster in practice. It demonstrates remarkable domain transferability across models and datasets. The paper also compares against EigenScore (a spectral baseline using eigenvalues of generation embedding covariance matrices) and outperforms it — this confirms that topological features of attention graphs provide complementary information beyond existing spectral approaches. We should test TOHA as an additional baseline on our HotpotQA dataset where our spectral method failed (59.5%) — TOHA achieves 71–80% there. Code repository URL not confirmed in available search results.
public_code_url: [uncertain] — not confirmed in available search results; check arxiv or author pages
gpu_requirements: Single GPU sufficient for 7B–13B models; not explicitly stated but inference at 7B–13B scale is standard (single A100 or equivalent). No multi-GPU required.
sample_size_for_replication: 50 annotated samples for head selection; 25% of dataset for test set (variable per benchmark)
min_samples_for_replication: 50 annotated samples (hallucinated + grounded) sufficient for head selection with 'minor performance drop'
runtime_estimate: ~7x faster than SelfCheckGPT per inference measurement. Single forward pass + MTop-Div computation. No multi-generation sampling required. Exact per-sample time not reported.
cumulative_state_modeling: None — single-pass structural divergence between prompt and response token sets. No step-by-step or cumulative modeling.
